era_context: The year 572 CE falls within the reign of Byzantine Emperor Justin II (565–578 CE), the Sasanid Empire under Khosrow I Anushirvan (531–579 CE), and the declining Northern Zhou Dynasty in China. The Byzantine-Sasanid Wars were in their third decade, with both empires experiencing military and financial strain. In Europe, the Merovingian Frankish kingdoms were consolidating power. This period represents a moment of transition in world history, with major empires facing internal challenges and external pressures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: Byzantine-Sasanid War: Siege of Dara (Alternative Dating)
events.description: Some sources place the Siege of Dara in 572 CE rather than 575 CE. The fortress city of Dara, a key Byzantine stronghold on the Mesopotamian frontier, came under siege by Sasanid forces under Khosrow I. The siege was part of the broader Byzantine-Sasanid Wars. The fortress held strategic importance as a bulwark against Persian expansion into Byzantine Mesopotamia. The siege lasted several months and represented a critical moment in the ongoing struggle for control of the Fertile Crescent.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (Books I–VIII)

events.certainty_note: The Siege of Dara is attested in Byzantine chronicles, but its precise dating is debated among scholars. Some place it in 572 CE, others in 573, 574, or 575 CE. The event occurred within this range; the exact year is uncertain.
cross_references: Related to broader Byzantine-Sasanid conflict spanning 540–591 CE

events.title: Frankish Kingdom Consolidation: Sigebert I's Campaigns
events.description: King Sigebert I of Austrasia was engaged in military campaigns to consolidate Austrasian power in 572 CE. Sigebert was one of the more capable Merovingian rulers and sought to expand Austrasian influence at the expense of his brother Chilperic I of Neustria. These campaigns were part of the broader Merovingian civil wars that characterized the period. Sigebert's military success made him a formidable power broker in Frankish politics.

sources.name: Gregory of Tours, History of the Franks

events.certainty_note: Gregory of Tours provides detailed accounts of Frankish politics in the 570s. His work is the primary source for this period.

events.title: Northern Zhou Dynasty Decline Accelerates
events.description: The Northern Zhou Dynasty was experiencing accelerating decline in 572 CE. Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou was aging, and internal factionalism was increasing. The dynasty's military and administrative capacity was deteriorating, and the rise of Yang Jian as a power broker was becoming more pronounced. By 572, the Northern Zhou's collapse was clearly imminent, though the dynasty would not formally fall until 581 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou (r. 561–578 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The decline of Northern Zhou in the 570s is well-documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Leads to Sui Dynasty unification in 581 CE

events.title: Avar Pressure on Byzantine Balkans
events.description: The Avars, a nomadic confederation that had established themselves in the Pannonian Plain, continued to exert pressure on Byzantine-controlled territories in the Balkans. These raids and incursions were part of a sustained pattern of Avar expansion in the 570s. The Avars were increasingly asserting dominance over the region and would become a major threat to Byzantine security in subsequent decades. These incursions prompted Byzantine military responses and diplomatic negotiations.
events.certainty_note: Avar pressure on the Balkans in the 570s is well-attested in Byzantine sources, but specific events in 572 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Siege of Dara is highly disputed among scholars. Some place it in 572 CE, others in 573, 574, or 575 CE. This uncertainty reflects the limitations of Byzantine chronology in the 6th century. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the other events listed, but precision is limited.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 572 CE is moderate, with strong documentation for the Byzantine Empire, Frankish kingdoms, and China, but sparse sources for other regions. Byzantine sources (Menander, Theophylact) are later compilations and reflect court perspectives. Gregory of Tours is the primary source for Frankish history, written from a Frankish Christian perspective. Chinese sources are systematic but reflect dynastic historiography. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The dating of specific events in the 570s is often uncertain, reflecting the limitations of 6th-century chronology.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Byzantine-Sasanid Wars (540–591 CE)
Northern Zhou decline led to Sui Dynasty unification
